The next session of you (or another engineer) will pick this up cold.

[1-2 sentences from the original spec]

## What is now done
[Bullet list — file by file, what changed]

## What is in progress
[What's half-finished, where, and why it stopped]

## What is NOT done yet
[Remaining tasks in order]

[Things I should answer before you continue]

[A ready-to-run prompt that gets a new session up to speed in one message]","content":"```

You handle (the 80%):
> Happy-path implementation

I handle (the 20% - you stop and flag these):
> Authentication and authorization checks

> Database migrations and destructive queries

Then wait. Don't guess.","content":"```

You are no longer the engineer who wrote this code.
You are a hostile senior reviewer on day one of a security audit.

Re-read the diff above. Find:

1. BUGS - what breaks under real inputs?
2. EDGE CASES - null, empty, very large, very small, concurrent, malformed
3. SECURITY - injection, auth bypass, data leaks, unsafe deserialization, secrets in logs
4. PERFORMANCE - N+1 queries, unbounded loops, blocking I/O on hot paths
5. MAINTAINABILITY - what will confuse the next engineer in 6 months
6. ASSUMPTIONS - what did the original author assume that isn't guaranteed

Output format:
[SEVERITY: critical | high | medium | low]
[CATEGORY]
[Issue in one sentence]
[Exact line / file]
[Proposed fix in 1-3 lines of code]

Rank by severity. Do not be polite. Do not hedge. If the code is fine, say "no issues found" - but only if you mean it.","content":"```
DESTROY MODE

You are no longer the engineer who wrote this code.
You are a hostile senior reviewer on day one of a security audit.

Re-read the diff above. Find:

1. BUGS - what breaks under real inputs?
2. EDGE CASES - null, empty, very large, very small, concurrent, malformed
3. SECURITY - injection, auth bypass, data leaks, unsafe deserialization, secrets in logs
4. PERFORMANCE - N+1 queries, unbounded loops, blocking I/O on hot paths
5. MAINTAINABILITY - what will confuse the next engineer in 6 months
6. ASSUMPTIONS - what did the original author assume that isn't guaranteed

Output format:
[SEVERITY: critical | high | medium | low]
[CATEGORY]
[Issue in one sentence]
[Exact line / file]
[Proposed fix in 1-3 lines of code]

Rank by severity. Do not be polite. Do not hedge. If the code is fine, say "no issues found" - but only if you mean it.
```"},{"language":"none","code":"COMMIT POLICY (paste into CLAUDE.md)

> Commit after every passing test or working unit
> One logical change per commit — never bundle
> Commit message format:
    type(scope): what changed in <50 chars
    [blank line]
    why this change exists (not what — the diff shows what)
    [blank line]
    refs: #issue or "spec line X"
> Never squash on merge. We use bisect.
> If a commit can't be described in one sentence, it's two commits.","content":"```
COMMIT POLICY (paste into CLAUDE.md)

> Commit after every passing test or working unit
> One logical change per commit — never bundle
> Commit message format:
    type(scope): what changed in <50 chars
    [blank line]
    why this change exists (not what — the diff shows what)
    [blank line]
    refs: #issue or "spec line X"
> Never squash on merge. We use bisect.
> If a commit can't be described in one sentence, it's two commits.
```"},{"language":"none","code":"# PROJECT CONTEXT

Respond in exactly this order. Nothing else.

1. DIFF
   Unified diff format only. Show only what changed.
   No "here is the updated file" full re-pastes.

2. WHAT CHANGED (max 5 bullets)
   Plain English. One bullet per logical change.

3. WHAT I DID NOT CHANGE (max 3 bullets)
   Things I considered but left alone, with a 1-line reason.

4. TESTS YOU SHOULD ADD
   List the test cases by name. Do not write them unless I ask.

5. RISKS
   What could break in production. Be specific.

If you cannot produce a clean diff (e.g., new file), say so and produce the file with a clear filename header.","content":"```

```"}]},"preview_text":"AI coding tools have been mainstream for two years. 92% of developers use them every day.
And yet METR's study on experienced engineers found AI made them 19% slower - while they were sure they'd been","plain_text":"92% of US developers use AI coding tools every day. Nearly half of all new code shipped this year was written by AI.
And yet - the most rigorous study we have (METR's trial on experienced engineers) found AI made them 19% slower at real work.
The same developers were sure they'd been sped up by 20%.
That's a 39-point gap between what people feel and what the clock actually shows.
It's not a contradiction. Both realities are real. Which one shows up in your terminal comes down to a few rules almost nobody is following.
This isn't a think piece. It's the workflow.
14 rules. 3 phases: setup, execution, compounding. Most take 30 seconds. None need new tools.
The engineers already turning the slowdown into a speedup use all 14.
 
Phase One — Setup. Where 80% of the outcome is already decided.
Rule 1 — Write a 5-line spec before your first prompt.
The single most common reason vibe coding sessions go sideways is that they start with vibes. You type a vague request into Cursor or Claude Code. The model fills the missing 90% of the spec with its best guess. Then you spend the next forty minutes fighting the gap between what you said and what you meant.
Andrej Karpathy — the person who coined "vibe coding" in February 2025 — said in 2026 that the term is now obsolete. He calls what comes next agentic engineering: spec-driven, planning-first, where the AI is treated as a stochastic specialist that needs explicit constraints to perform. Every senior engineer publishing workflows in 2026 is converging on the same point. Stop prompting. Start specifying.
Before your first request to the model, fill in these five lines:
Goal: in one sentence, what is the end state when this is shipped. Success criteria: a checklist of 3-5 observable signals that prove the goal was met. Inputs: what data, files, or APIs the code receives. Outputs: what it returns or writes. Out of scope: what the model is explicitly forbidden from touching.
Paste this at the top of every new task. It feels slow. It saves you cycles by the third turn. The model now has a target instead of a vibe.
Rule 2 — Declare what you are NOT building.
Scope drift is the largest invisible time tax in AI-assisted work. The model is trained to be helpful, which means it will quietly add features, expand tests, refactor adjacent files, and introduce dependencies you didn't ask for. Every addition compounds into review work you didn't plan. By the time you've audited the third unrequested change, the supposed speedup is gone.
The fix is to declare the negative space at the same time you declare the goal. Most people define what they want. Almost nobody defines what they don't want. That asymmetry is where hours bleed.
After your 5-line spec, add this block:
NOT building in this turn: list 3 to 5 things explicitly out of scope. NOT touching these files: list any file the model is forbidden from modifying. NOT adding: list patterns, libraries, or abstractions that should not appear in the output. If you think something belongs outside this scope, stop and ask before adding it.
This one rule, applied consistently, eliminates more wasted cycles than any other prompt in this article. The model becomes a focused contractor with a fixed brief instead of a helpful generalist trying to impress you.
Rule 3 — Pick your model by task class, not by hype.
Most people default to the most expensive model for every task because it feels safer. It isn't. METR's data showed that experienced developers only accepted fewer than 44% of AI generations — and the rejected ones still cost them time to review. A more powerful model on a poorly matched task produces more confident slop, not less slop.
Match the model to the work. There are three tiers and three rules:
For architecture, ambiguous problems, and any task that requires holding the entire system in working memory at once — use the frontier reasoning models. Opus, GPT-5, Gemini Ultra. These are the models you pay for when judgment matters more than speed.
For clean implementation work where the problem is well-specified and the solution path is mostly known — use mid-tier models. Sonnet, GPT-5 standard tier. Fast, capable, cheap enough to throw at multiple iterations without flinching.
For boilerplate, test scaffolding, doc generation, type definitions, and refactor proposals — use the small models. Haiku, Gemini Flash, GPT-4.1 mini. Work where speed and cost matter more than judgment.
If you can't tell which tier a task belongs to, that itself is the signal: the task isn't specified well enough yet. Go back to Rule 1, finish the spec, then pick the model. The wrong-model penalty is real and silent.
Rule 4 — Build your CLAUDE.md (or .cursorrules) before writing a single line.
METR's fifth identified factor in the 19% slowdown was implicit repository context. AI doesn't know your conventions. It doesn't know the auth module is being migrated. It doesn't know /lib/legacy is deprecated. It doesn't know billing changes need review from a specific person. Senior engineers carry hundreds of tiny rules in their heads. The model carries zero.
A project-level context file fixes most of this. Anthropic's official documentation on Claude Code best practices recommends keeping a CLAUDE.md in the repo root. Cursor calls it .cursorrules. Every modern AI coding tool now has an equivalent primitive. The format varies. The job is the same: load full project context every session so you stop re-explaining your codebase every time you open a chat.
This is the highest-leverage artifact in the entire article. Write it once. Use it forever.
Use this template:
Stack: list languages, frameworks, runtime versions. Conventions: how files are named, where things live, what the formatter expects. Patterns we use: name the design patterns, libraries, or idioms standard in this repo. Patterns we don't use: list anti-patterns or libraries that should never appear. Files to be careful with: list the load-bearing files and explain why each is sensitive. Known gotchas: list 3 to 5 traps the model will fall into without warning. When in doubt, default to: state the safe default behavior in plain language.
Twelve lines. Drop it in the repo root. Reference it explicitly in your first prompt every session: "Read CLAUDE.md before doing anything." You'll feel the difference immediately. Code that came out feeling foreign now feels like the codebase.
Phase Two — Execution. Where most hours are lost.
Rule 5 — Force the model to ask before it codes.
This is the single highest-leverage prompt in this article. The 44% acceptance rate METR documented is not a model intelligence problem. It is a guessing problem. The model is filling in missing context with its best guess, then you spend twenty minutes reviewing code that solved a slightly different problem than the one you had.
The fix is to remove the guessing entirely. Make the model surface what it doesn't know before it commits to an approach.
Paste this prompt before any non-trivial task:
Before writing any code, ask me the 5 highest-leverage questions whose answers would change your approach. For each question, briefly explain why the answer matters and how the answers would change what you build. Do not generate any code until I have answered all 5.
The first time you run this, the questions will surprise you. The model will surface assumptions you didn't know you were making. Two minutes of answering questions saves you four iterations of "almost right" code. This rule alone closes most of the distance between the 19% group and the 4x group.
If you do nothing else in this article, do this.
Rule 6 — One file per turn. No exceptions until you've earned them.
Multi-file edits in a single turn are where AI-assisted work quietly breaks. The model loses track of which change belongs where. Imports duplicate. Type signatures drift across files. You end up with a working "thing" that breaks somewhere you didn't think to test. By the time you find the issue, you've burned more time than you saved.
The fix is mechanical. Lock each turn to one file, and force the model to surface any cross-file impacts before they happen.
Use this constraint at the start of any session touching shared state:
Edit only the file I name. If you believe changes to other files are required, do not make them. Instead, list each file that would need to change, briefly explain what change is required and why, and stop. I will authorize each file individually in a separate turn.
When the model wants to touch four files, three of them usually shouldn't be touched. The pause is the value. Treat any session where the model "had to" edit unrelated files as a session that needs review before commit.
Rule 7 — Ship the working 80%. Forbid AI from the last 20%.
METR's task-level analysis revealed a specific pattern: AI wins on the bulk of work — scaffolding, main paths, happy cases, type definitions, test stubs. AI loses systematically on edges — auth, billing, error handling, integration boundaries, anything user-facing-critical, anything that writes to production data. The 80% is where the speedup lives. The 20% is where the 19% slowdown is born, because AI produces wrong-looking-right code in exactly the situations where wrong-looking-right code is most expensive.
The 4x group ships the 80% in an hour and spends the rest of the day on the 20% by hand. The 19% group lets AI write everything, then spends three days finding the silent bugs.
Make the line explicit. Add this block to your CLAUDE.md so the rule is loaded every session:
AI is authorized to handle: scaffolding, main code paths, type definitions, test stubs, documentation drafts, refactor proposals. AI is NOT authorized to touch without explicit human approval: authentication, authorization, billing, payment flows, anything writing to production data, anything user-facing-critical, anything involving cryptographic operations. When work crosses an unauthorized boundary, stop and surface that fact in the response.
This single rule is the difference between AI shipping you a working product and AI shipping you a working-looking product with a security incident hiding in the auth flow.
Rule 8 — Run "destroy mode" on every implementation before you commit.
Every commercial frontier model is a sycophant by default. They agree. They expand. They elaborate. They will tell you your code is "well-structured" three minutes after generating the race condition that takes you a week to find. This is a product decision, not a capability decision — the models are tuned to be liked.
You don't need them to be liked. You need them to be right. The good news is the same model that just wrote the code, when prompted differently, will find real problems in it.
Run this immediately after any meaningful piece of code ships:
You just wrote this code. Now act as a senior engineer reviewing it in production for the first time. You did not write it. Your job is to find every bug, every edge case it ignores, every assumption that could break under load or in failure modes. Be brutal. After you've listed the issues, rank the top 3 by likelihood multiplied by blast radius. For each of those 3, propose a concrete fix and the test that would catch the regression.
This is the cheapest code review you will ever get. It catches issues the original prompt was too friendly to surface. Stack this with Rule 5 — ask first, then destroy after — and you've replicated the substance of a senior engineer pair-programming with you, at the cost of two extra prompts per task.
Rule 9 — Trash the conversation when context drifts. Don't compress.
When a session crosses about 50% of the model's context window, two things happen. You start paying double the tokens per turn because the model is processing more history. And earlier context bleeds into unrelated turns — you ask about file A and get answers shaped by code from file B that you discussed two hours ago.
Most people try to compress. Compression is the wrong instinct. It's heroic engineering that buys you maybe 20% more session before the same problems return. Aggressive resets are cheaper, cleaner, and the move every senior practitioner converged on in 2026.
When you feel the drift, run this once, then start a fresh session:
Produce a handoff document for the next session. Include: what we built, what is working and committed, what is broken or in progress, what decisions we made together that need to carry forward, what's next in priority order, and any context the next session needs to know that isn't already in CLAUDE.md.
Paste the handoff into a session-notes.md file or directly into CLAUDE.md for the duration of the project. Open a new chat. Continue with full context, half the token bill, and zero context bleed. The next session starts fresh and oriented.
Rule 10 — Review the diff. Never the output.
Most people review the final code the model produced. The 4x group reviews the change — what was added, what was deleted, what got quietly mutated in passing. The distinction sounds small. It is the entire game.
When you review final code, you see what's there. You miss what got removed, renamed, or refactored "while it was at it." Every "I also cleaned up X" is a chance for the model to silently change behavior in a function you didn't think to check. By the time the regression shows up in production, the diff that caused it is buried under twenty more commits.
Force diff output as a habit, not a one-time prompt:
Output all changes as a unified diff before any explanation. Show only changed hunks, not the full files. After I approve the diff, generate the list of tests I should write to lock this behavior in, and the list of existing tests that should now pass that didn't before. Do not produce any prose until the diff is shown.
The diff catches the silent changes. The follow-up question catches the missing tests. Together they replace 80% of what a careful PR review used to do.
Phase Three — Compounding. Where the 4x lives.
Rule 11 — Save every working prompt as a skill.
One-time prompts lose 100% of their value the second the session ends. The 4x group treats prompts the way good engineers treat code: anything that worked twice becomes a saved, named, reusable asset that runs across sessions and across projects.
Every major tool now has a primitive for this. Claude Skills launched in late 2025 and turned saved prompts into portable agents that run across Chat, Cowork, and Claude Code. Cursor has rules. Custom GPTs have instructions. Public libraries like clskillshub.com host nearly 800 community-shared skills. The infrastructure is built. Most people are still re-typing the same prompts daily.
When you find a prompt that worked twice, promote it. Format:
Name: short verb-based name. Trigger: the situation where this skill should run. Prompt: the exact prompt text that worked, with placeholders for variables. Inputs: what the skill needs from the user. Success signal: how you'll know the skill worked.
Save it as a .md file in a skills/ folder in your project, or as a real Claude Skill if your tool supports it. Next month, when you hit the same situation, you don't re-derive the prompt. You run the skill. This is the compounding step that separates linear users from exponential ones.
Rule 12 — Ship daily. Commit small. Squash never.
SemiAnalysis estimated in early 2026 that 4% of all public GitHub commits were already being authored by Claude Code, with a projected trajectory of 20%+ by year-end. That trajectory did not come from people batching 8-hour vibe sessions and dumping a megacommit at the end. It came from developers making small, AI-assisted, human-reviewed commits constantly.
Small commits compound advantages. They give you a clean git bisect when something breaks. They let you ship the 80% without waiting for the 20%. They keep the model's context tight, because each session has narrow, declared scope. They produce a paper trail of AI-assisted decisions that future-you (or your team) can audit.
Adopt this discipline as a rule, not a preference:
Every session ends with one of two outputs: a clean commit pushed to the branch, or a handoff document saved to session-notes.md. No half-finished branches. No "I'll squash later." No "I'll clean it up before the PR." The unit of work that is done ships. The unit of work that is not done is handed off cleanly. Move on.
The 19% group works in 4-hour heroic sessions and produces one PR a week. The 4x group works in 30-minute focused units and produces eight.
Rule 13 — Stop writing prompts. Start writing loops.
This is the shift the most senior engineers have already made, and it's the single biggest thesis change in AI engineering between 2025 and 2026. Shopify's Head of Engineering put it bluntly: "AI writes the code. AI reviews the code. Your job is to write the loops around it." The creator of Claude Code said the same thing in different words: "I don't write prompts anymore. I have loops running that prompt Claude. My job is to write the loops."
The unit of engineering work in 2026 is no longer the snippet. It's the loop — a structured workflow with a trigger, an action, a validation step, and an escalation path. Skills are static. Loops are running.
Anytime you find yourself prompting the model twice for similar work, stop and write the loop instead. Four parts:
Trigger: the condition that kicks the loop off (new bug filed, new PR opened, file changed). Action: the prompt or sequence the model runs. Validation: the automated check that determines if the result is acceptable (test passes, lint clean, type check green, human ack). Escalation: what happens when the validation fails (retry with refined prompt, fall back to a more capable model, surface to human).
Bug-fix workflows are loops. Refactor proposals are loops. Documentation passes are loops. Once you start seeing the loops, your output stops scaling linearly with how fast you type and starts scaling with how many loops you have running in parallel.
Rule 14 — Measure cycle time. Throw out everything else.
Every legacy productivity metric is now broken. Lines of code, PR count, commits per week, story points — all distorted by AI output volume. A junior with no taste can ship 10,000 lines of slop in a day. That is not productivity. That is technical debt manufactured at industrial speed.
There is exactly one metric AI tools should be evaluated against: cycle time from issue opened to issue closed, verified in production. Everything else is vanity.
Run this experiment on yourself for one week:
For every task this week, log four timestamps in a simple spreadsheet: when the task was scoped, when the first commit shipped, when the PR was merged, when the work was verified working in production. Tag each task with which model and tools were used. Tag each with a difficulty score, 1 to 5.
After seven days you will know exactly where AI helps your work and where it doesn't. The bottlenecks are almost never where people expect. Most developers find their AI-assisted coding time dropped 60% — and their review, verification, and rework time tripled. That's the 19% slowdown made visible. Once you can see it on a spreadsheet, you can fix it.
The Two Profiles
Identical tools. Identical models. Identical IDEs. Two outcomes that diverge by a factor of twenty.
The 19% group writes vague prompts. Accepts the first output. Stays in long sessions. Edits AI code without reading diffs. Never saves a prompt as a skill. Measures success by lines shipped. Trusts the model most when it agrees with them.
The 4x group writes specs before prompts. Demands clarifying questions before code. Locks scope, kills sessions before they drift, reviews diffs not output, runs adversarial reviews on their own code, saves working prompts as skills, writes loops not snippets, and measures success by cycle time alone.
The two groups don't have different tools. They have different rules. The 19% slowdown isn't the future of AI coding. It's the present cost of using 2026 tools with 2024 habits.
The fourteen rules above are the difference. None of them require new tools. None of them require new models. Most of them take less than 30 seconds to apply.
Start with the four in Phase One. The setup decides 80% of the outcome, and almost nobody does the setup. The other ten get you the rest of the way.
Vibe coding doesn't make you a better engineer. It makes you a better operator. The engineers who survive the next two years aren't the ones who write the best prompts. They're the ones who built the best loops. The 19% slowdown is real. So is the 4x speedup. The only thing that decides which one shows up in your terminal is whether you treat AI like a vending machine — or like a team."}
